[03:30–04:00]

and security, special education, pre K, early literacy screenings, and math programming, and also
teacher preparation programming. What we are watching and monitoring closely is what types of
strings might be attached to that money. And so to the extent that the money is flowing.

[04:30–05:00]
That the money is flexible, we're able to really operate given the needs of our specific school

interesting part of how this all rolls out. The Senate and the House are still working through the
details of House Bill 2, and it has not been yet signed by the governor, but we will continue to
keep you all updated on that. And then lastly, the other piece of legislation that we're following
closely is House Bill 4. Many of you probably know there has been an outcry across the state for the
past several years over the star.

[05:00–05:30]
test. I don't think that educators, parents, students, board members, any of us are really thrilled
with that high-stakes one day test and the toll that it takes on our students and our staff and our
teachers. And House Bill 4 actually is a piece of legislation that would do away with the STAR test
and would replace it with a nationally norm reference test, which really provides significant, much
needed accountability reforms for school districts. So we would still be rated as a school district.
We would still be accountable, which we want, but it would replace the star test with a nationally
norm.
